prompt.name: optimized-tests
prompt.content:
│ │ # Storybook Setup │ ## Project Info │ | Property | Value | │ |----------|-------| │ | Version | 10.4.0-alpha.10 | │ | Renderer | @storybook/react | │ | Framework | @storybook/react-vite | │ | Builder | @storybook/builder-vite | │ | Config Dir | `.storybook` | │ | CSF Format | CSF Factory | │ | Addons | @storybook/addon-onboarding, @storybook/addon-themes, │ @storybook/addon-docs, @storybook/addon-designs, │ @storybook/addon-vitest, @storybook/addon-a11y, │ storybook-addon-pseudo-states, @chromatic-com/storybook | │ Attention: The following instructions must be followed in order to │ successfully set up Storybook in this project. Do not skip steps or │ attempt to do them out of order. │ Your goal is to make Storybook fully functional in this project by │ analyzing the codebase, │ configuring the preview with the right decorators, and writing stories │ for some components. │ The end state should be a Storybook where any component — from a small │ button to a full page — can be added without story-specific │ workarounds. All necessary providers, CSS, browser state, and network │ mocks should live in the shared preview so that just rendering the │ component in the story is enough. │ After each created story, run Vitest to verify it renders. │ If the test fails, read the error, fix the issue, and re-run until it │ passes before moving on. │ - Copy real patterns from the codebase │ - Keep the app code unchanged │ - Put the default setup in `.storybook/preview.tsx` │ - Keep app mocking and runtime setup in `.storybook/preview.tsx`, not │ in the stories │ ### Storybook Documentation Reference │ Use the following references to look up Storybook APIs, concepts, or │ examples: │ - Full docs index: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/llms.txthttps://storybook.js.org/llms.txt]8;; │ - See code snippets only with codeOnly=true param e.g. │ ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=ts&codeOnly=truehttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=ts&codeOnly=true]8;; │ Key documentation pages for this task: │ - Writing stories: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories...]8;; │ - Decorators: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/decorators.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/deco...]8;; │ - Args: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/args.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/args.md?re...]8;; │ - Play functions: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/play-function.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/...]8;; │ - Vitest integration: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-tests/vitest-plugin.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-test...]8;; │ Fetch these URLs directly when you need guidance on Storybook APIs or │ patterns. │ ### Step 1: Analyze the codebase │ Read enough of the app to understand the full runtime environment │ before writing any stories. │ Do not stop at `main.tsx` or `App.tsx`. │ Follow imports into providers, pages, hooks, and shared components │ until you know: │ - which providers exist │ - which CSS files are injected │ - which queries fetch data │ - which browser-state reads happen │ - which portals and portal roots exist │ - which pages and components show the real usage patterns │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // src/main.tsx │ import "./index.css"; │ import App from "./App"; │ import { SessionProvider } from "./contexts/SessionContext"; │ createRoot(document.getElementById("root")!).render( │ │ │ , │ ); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should copy: │ - the `index.css` import │ - the `SessionProvider` │ - the same provider order │ Example of tracing the app deeper: │ ```tsx │ // src/App.tsx │ function App() { │ const { products, loadMoreProducts } = useProducts(); │ const { currentUser, signOut } = useSession(); │ // ... │ } │ ``` │ ```ts │ // src/hooks/useProducts.ts │ const response = await fetch(apiBaseUrl + "/products?page=1"); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // src/hooks/useTheme.ts │ const savedTheme = localStorage.getItem("theme"); │ ``` │ That means the default Storybook setup should discover and prepare: │ - provider state │ - MSW handlers for queries │ - browser-state values that are actually read during render │ ### Step 2: Build one default app environment in preview │ Set up Storybook once so most stories work without story-specific │ setup. │ Start with the smallest faithful environment: │ - the real provider tree │ - the real root CSS │ - seeded browser state if the app reads it during render │ - MSW for network/data queries │ It is fine to seed browser state such as `localStorage`, │ `sessionStorage`, and cookies when the app reads them during render. │ Seed only the specific app-owned keys and values you need. │ Do not clear all `localStorage`, `sessionStorage`, or cookies, and do │ not reset Storybook's own state. │ Do not mock or redefine the browser runtime itself. │ The stories run in Vitest browser mode, so the real browser │ environment should already exist. │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles │ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; │ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; │ import { SessionProvider } from '../src/contexts/SessionContext'; │ export default definePreview({ │ decorators: [ │ (Story) => ( │ │ │ │ ), │ ], │ async beforeEach() { │ localStorage.setItem('theme', 'dark'); │ localStorage.setItem('sidebar:open', 'true'); │ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ Use this same idea for: │ - providers │ - root CSS │ - browser state │ - dates, and if the app logic depends on them during render then │ always use `mockdate` │ Example with the `mockdate` package: │ ```tsx │ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; │ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; │ export default definePreview({ │ async beforeEach() { │ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ ### Step 3: Support portals with preview-body.html │ If the app uses portals, copy that setup into Storybook too. │ Look for patterns like: │ - `createPortal(...)` │ - modal, dialog, drawer, popover, tooltip, toast, or dropdown portal │ components │ - hard-coded roots such as `#portal-root`, `#modal-root`, │ `#drawer-root`, or `#toast-root` │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // real component │ return createPortal(, │ document.getElementById("portal-root")!); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should create the same portal root in │ `.storybook/preview-body.html`: │ ```html │ │
│ ``` │ If the app uses multiple portal roots, create all of them there: │ ```html │ │
│
│
│ ``` │ If a library portals directly to `document.body`, do not add extra │ roots for it. │ Make sure the copied page shell, CSS, and layout still allow overlays, │ fixed positioning, and z-index stacking to render correctly. │ ### Step 4: Mock side effects globally │ All network/data queries should be handled by the default Storybook │ environment. │ - Always use `msw-storybook-addon` for query mocking. │ - If you introduce MSW, run `npx msw init ./public --save` to create │ the worker file. │ - Make sure Storybook serves `./public` as a static dir so │ `mockServiceWorker.js` is available. │ - Do not mock `fetch` directly. │ - Network/data queries should return deterministic mock data. │ - If you need to change dependencies, first check the lockfile and use │ that package manager for the change. │ Example of copying a real fetch pattern into shared handlers: │ ```ts │ // real app hook │ const response = await fetch( │ apiBaseUrl + │ "/products?" + │ new URLSearchParams({ │ page: "1", │ sort: "featured", │ }), │ ); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/msw-handlers.ts │ import { http, HttpResponse } from "msw"; │ export const mswHandlers = { │ products: [ │ http.get("]8;;https://api.example.com/products",https://api.example.com/products",]8;; │ () => │ HttpResponse.json({ │ items: [ │ { │ id: "product-1", │ name: "Example product", │ description: "Mock product description", │ imageUrl: │ "]8;;https://images.example.com/product.jpg",https://images.example.com/product.jpg",]8;; │ price: 42, │ }, │ ], │ }), │ ), │ ], │ }; │ ``` │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; │ import { initialize, mswLoader } from 'msw-storybook-addon'; │ import { mswHandlers } from './msw-handlers'; │ initialize({ │ onUnhandledRequest: 'bypass', │ }); │ export default definePreview({ │ loaders: [mswLoader], │ parameters: { │ msw: { │ handlers: mswHandlers, │ }, │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/main.ts │ import type { StorybookConfig } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ const config: StorybookConfig = { │ staticDirs: ["../public"], │ }; │ export default config; │ ``` │ Keep these mocks global. │ Do not put fetch mocks in individual stories. │ Only add handlers for requests that the shared preview setup or the │ stories actually use. │ Do not add catch-all handlers that can hide unrelated failures. │ If the defaults are not enough, improve the shared default setup │ instead. │ Seed browser state when needed, but do not mock `window`, `document`, │ `navigator`, observers, or similar runtime APIs. │ The only exception is `mockdate` when date-based rendering exists. │ ### Step 5: Write stories │ Try to find around 10 good candidate components for story files. │ Write colocated stories for top-level components, from low-level │ reusable components up to page components. │ Write up to 10 story files, or fewer only if the codebase clearly has │ fewer meaningful targets. │ The stories should use JSX copied from real usage patterns in: │ - pages │ - app shells │ - routes │ - tests │ - existing feature code │ As a rule of thumb, each story file should have around 3 story exports │ when the component or page has enough meaningful states. │ It can have more when the real usage supports it, up to 10 story │ exports in one file. │ Always show all imports explicitly in story and preview files. │ Do not rely on omitted or implied imports in examples or generated │ code. │ #### Story tags │ Every story meta must include the `ai-generated` tag to identify │ AI-created stories: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { SomeComponent } from './SomeComponent'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const Default = meta.story({ │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ If a story could not be fully fixed after the self-healing loop (the │ test still fails │ or the rendering is incomplete), add the `needs-work` tag alongside │ `ai-generated`: │ ```ts │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated', 'needs-work'], │ }); │ ``` │ #### Args vs render │ For simple components where props drive the state, prefer `args` │ stories — no `render` function needed: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { Button } from './Button'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: Button, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const Primary = meta.story({ │ args: { │ variant: 'primary', │ children: 'Save', │ }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i │ })).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }); │ export const Disabled = meta.story({ │ args: { │ variant: 'primary', │ disabled: true, │ children: 'Save', │ }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeDisabled(); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ Use `render` when the story needs composition — wrapping the component │ in layout, combining multiple components, or passing children as JSX: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { Button } from './Button'; │ import { Card } from './Card'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: Button, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const InsideCard = meta.story({ │ render: () => ( │ │ │ │ ), │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i │ })).toBeVisible(); │ await userEvent.click(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i })); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ Keep app mocking and runtime setup in preview, not in the stories. │ Do not build large story-specific harnesses. │ Do not write story files for subcomponents, hooks, contexts, or │ helpers. │ Do not create new application components. │ Do not add a custom `title`. │ Do not stop after only a few easy targets if the codebase has more │ meaningful components or pages available. │ ### Step 6: Write a play function for every story │ Every named story export must have a `play` function. │ The `play` function is not optional, even for simple stories. │ The purpose of the `play` function is to prove that the story actually │ works in the copied Storybook environment: │ - the story renders something real and non-empty │ - the decorators provide the needed context │ - the CSS is applied well enough for the intended state to be visible │ - the MSW mocks or seeded browser state are actually being used │ - important interactions, async loading states, and portals behave │ correctly │ Use `play` functions to verify behavior, not just to click around. │ A story without assertions is incomplete. │ Use tools from `storybook/test` such as: │ - `expect` │ - `waitFor` │ Prefer `canvas` and `userEvent` from the `play` context. │ Do not destructure `canvasElement` just to create `const canvas = │ within(canvasElement)`. │ Do not import `userEvent` from `storybook/test`; use `userEvent` from │ the `play` context instead. │ Only use `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when you need to query outside │ the canvas, such as for portals. │ Example: │ ```tsx │ import type { StoryObj } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ export const FilledForm: Story = { │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ const emailInput = canvas.getByLabelText("email", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(emailInput, "example-email@email.com", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const passwordInput = canvas.getByLabelText("password", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(passwordInput, "ExamplePassword", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const submitButton = canvas.getByRole("button"); │ await userEvent.click(submitButton); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ The assertions should match the real pattern you copied: │ - for provider-backed stories, assert the provider-dependent UI │ appears correctly │ - for mocked-data stories, wait for the mocked data to appear and │ assert on it │ - for CSS-sensitive states, assert on visibility, text layout, │ class-driven states, or meaningful computed styles │ - for routing or navigation stories, assert the routed state or │ navigation outcome │ - for portal stories, query from `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when │ the UI renders outside the canvas │ Examples of useful checks: │ - a themed button has the expected label and is visibly enabled or │ disabled │ - a modal opened through a decorator or provider is visible in the │ portal root │ - mocked API data appears in the page instead of a loading spinner │ forever │ - a selected tab actually shows the selected panel │ - a toast, alert, or badge has the expected accessible text and visual │ state │ - a CSS class or computed style confirms the real state that matters │ ### Step 7: Prove CSS is loaded in exactly one story named `CssCheck` │ In exactly one story, named `CssCheck`, assert a component-specific │ computed style. `toBeVisible` passes on an unstyled component; a │ concrete style value proves the shared preview loaded the app's CSS. │ Pick a visually distinctive component, read a styling value from its │ source, and assert it with `getComputedStyle`: │ ```tsx │ export const CssCheck: Story = { │ args: { children: "Submit" }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ const button = canvas.getByRole("button", { name: /submit/i }); │ // PrimaryButton uses bg-blue-600 — fails if Tailwind / global CSS did │ not load. │ await expect(getComputedStyle(button).backgroundColor).toBe("rgb(37, │ 99, 235)"); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ ### Step 8: Cover the patterns you found │ Write stories for the real patterns in the codebase, for example: │ - a low-level reusable component in real JSX usage │ - a provider-backed component │ - a browser-state-backed component │ - a fetched-data component │ - a real page component │ Use `App.tsx` to inspect the real provider tree and usage patterns, │ but do not make a story for `App` when the codebase has actual page │ components. │ Example page story: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { ProductPage } from './ProductPage'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: ProductPage, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const Default = meta.story({ │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect( │ canvas.getByRole('heading', { name: /products/i }), │ ).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ ### Step 9: Verify both rendering and types │ As you work, verify the stories with Vitest: │ ```bash │ npx vitest --project storybook │ ``` │ Also verify types so you catch missing required props, broken imports, │ and preview typing issues. Run the same TypeScript command the project │ itself uses. │ ```bash │ │ ``` │ After verification passes, review every changed file and remove │ anything that is not needed for the final solution, especially debug │ fixes, overly broad mocks, unnecessary dependencies, and eval │ artifacts. │ Keep iterating until: │ - every story you wrote passes │ - every story you wrote has a meaningful passing `play` function │ - the changed stories and preview setup pass the project's real │ TypeScript check │ - the rendered output looks sensible │ - the default global mocked environment is strong enough that stories │ do not need manual fetch overrides │ - stories no longer fail because the shared preview setup and story │ JSX are fixed │ - all passing stories have `tags: ['ai-generated']` in their meta │ - any stories that still need work have `tags: ['ai-generated', │ 'needs-work']` in their meta

content.thinking: Let me start by analyzing the codebase to understand the project structure, providers, CSS, data fetching patterns, and components.

input.prompt:
Thoroughly explore this codebase to understand:
1. Project structure - list all directories and key files
2. Entry point (main.tsx) - what providers, CSS imports, and setup exists
3. App.tsx - what's the component tree, routing, providers
4. All context providers and what they provide
5. All hooks that fetch data (look for fetch, axios, useQuery, etc.)
6. All hooks that read browser state (localStorage, sessionStorage, cookies)
7. Portal usage (createPortal, modal roots, etc.)
8. CSS setup (tailwind, CSS modules, global CSS)
9. All page-level components
10. All reusable UI components
11. Package manager (look at lockfile)
12. TypeScript config and type-check command
13. Existing .storybook config

Be very thorough - read through all source files, follow all imports.

{
  "name": "@fanciers/echarts-react",
  "version": "0.0.4",
  "description": "ECharts <3 React",
  "author": "Tmk <i@tmk.im>",
  "keywords": [
    "echarts",
    "react",
    "component",
    "chart",
    "visualization"
  ],
  "license": "MIT",
  "type": "module",
  "sideEffects": false,
  "files": [
    "dist"
  ],
  "exports": {
    ".": {
      "types": "./dist/index.d.mts",
      "default": "./dist/index.mjs"
    }
  },
  "main": "./dist/index.mjs",
  "typings": "./dist/index.d.mts",
  "repository": {
    "url": "https://github.com/tmkx/echarts-react.git"
  },
  "scripts": {
    "dev": "storybook dev",
    "test": "vitest",
    "prepack": "tsdown",
    "lint": "tsc --noEmit",
    "storybook": "storybook dev -p 6006",
    "build-storybook": "storybook build",
    "chromatic": "chromatic --exit-zero-on-changes --only-changed",
    "vitest:storybook": "vitest run --project=storybook"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "@types/node": "^25.3.0",
    "@types/react": "^19.2.14",
    "@types/react-dom": "^19.2.3",
    "echarts": "^6.0.0",
    "react": "^19.2.4",
    "react-dom": "^19.2.4",
    "swr": "^2.4.0",
    "tsdown": "^0.20.3",
    "typescript": "^5.9.3",
    "vite": "8.0.0-beta.15",
    "vitest": "^4.0.18",
    "storybook": "0.0.0-pr-34595-sha-01068aa6",
    "@storybook/react-vite": "0.0.0-pr-34595-sha-01068aa6",
    "@chromatic-com/storybook": "^5.1.1",
    "@storybook/addon-vitest": "0.0.0-pr-34595-sha-01068aa6",
    "@storybook/addon-a11y": "0.0.0-pr-34595-sha-01068aa6",
    "@storybook/addon-docs": "0.0.0-pr-34595-sha-01068aa6",
    "@storybook/addon-onboarding": "0.0.0-pr-34595-sha-01068aa6",
    "playwright": "^1.58.2",
    "@vitest/browser-playwright": "4.0.18",
    "@vitest/coverage-v8": "4.0.18"
  },
  "peerDependencies": {
    "echarts": "^6.0.0",
    "react": ">=18.0.0",
    "react-dom": ">=18.0.0"
  },
  "packageManager": "pnpm@10.30.1",
  "pnpm": {
    "overrides": {
      "vite": "8.0.0-beta.15",
      "rolldown": "1.0.0-rc.5"
    }
  }
}

1→import type { StorybookConfig } from '@storybook/react-vite';
     2→
     3→const config: StorybookConfig = {
     4→  stories: [
     5→    '../src/**/*.mdx',
     6→    '../src/**/*.stories.@(js|jsx|mjs|ts|tsx)',
     7→    './eval-support/*.mdx',
     8→  ],
     9→  addons: [
    10→    '@chromatic-com/storybook',
    11→    '@storybook/addon-vitest',
    12→    '@storybook/addon-a11y',
    13→    '@storybook/addon-docs',
    14→    '@storybook/addon-onboarding',

Pages in Storybook

24→

25→ We recommend building UIs with a{' '} 26→ 27→ component-driven 28→ {' '} 29→ process starting with atomic components and ending with pages. 30→

31→

32→ Render pages with mock data. This makes it easy to build and review page states without 33→ needing to navigate to them in your app. Here are some handy patterns for managing page 34→ data in Storybook: 35→

36→
    37→
  • 38→ Use a higher-level connected component. Storybook helps you compose such data from the 39→ "args" of child component stories 40→
    • 41→
  • 42→ Assemble data in the page component from your services. You can mock these services out 43→ using Storybook. 44→
    • 45→
46→

47→ Get a guided tutorial on component-driven development at{' '} 48→ 49→ Storybook tutorials 50→ 51→ . Read more in the{' '} 52→ 53→ docs 54→ 55→ . 56→

  export { Brush, Calendar, Dataset, DataZoom, Geo, Graphic, Legend, MarkArea, MarkLine, MarkPoint, Matrix, Polar, SingleAxis, Timeline, Title, Toolbox, Tooltip, VisualMap } from './components.js';
  export { AxisBreak } from './features.js';

### 3. Core Architecture

**Project Type:** React component library for ECharts visualization components

### 4. Context Providers

**Single Context: ChartContext**
- **File:** `/Users/yannbraga/open-source/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-28T22-41-15Z-41b8dcb0/project/src/shared.tsx`
- **Purpose:** Manages chart options across child components
- **Interface:**
  ```typescript
  interface ChartContextValue {
    options: echarts.EChartsCoreOption[];
  }
  ```
- **Hooks:**
  - `useInitialChartContext()` - Creates initial context state
  - `useChartContext()` - Consumes context (throws in dev if not within ChartContext)
  - `useRegister(fn)` - Registers ECharts extensions
- **No Redux, Zustand, or other state management libraries**

### 5. Data Fetching Hooks

**No HTTP Data Fetching Hooks** - This is a presentational component library. No fetch, axios, SWR, or React Query imports found.

**Hook Summary:**
- `useInitialChartContext()` - State initialization
- `useChartContext()` - Context consumption
- `useRegister(fn)` - ECharts module registration

### 6. Browser State Hooks

**No Browser State Reading** - No hooks for:
- localStorage
- sessionStorage
- Cookies
- History API

All components are stateless/pure presentational except for the Page story which uses `React.useState` for demo purposes.

content.text:
Perfect! I have now read all the source files you requested. Here's a comprehensive summary of the complete codebase:

## Complete Source File Summary

### Core Files

**1. `/src/index.ts` - Main Entry Point**
- Exports all chart components (BarChart, EffectScatterChart, GraphChart, HeatmapChart, LineChart, PieChart, ScatterChart)
- Exports all UI components (Brush, Calendar, Dataset, DataZoom, Geo, Graphic, Legend, MarkArea, MarkLine, MarkPoint, Matrix, Polar, SingleAxis, Timeline, Title, Toolbox, Tooltip, VisualMap)
- Exports AxisBreak feature
- Exports echarts core and renderers (CanvasRenderer, SVGRenderer)
- Exports the echarts object itself for direct access

**2. `/src/shared.tsx` - Shared Context & Utilities**
```typescript
- ChartContextValue interface: { options: echarts.EChartsCoreOption[] }
- ChartContext: React Context for chart options management
- defaultSetOptionOpt: { notMerge: false, lazyUpdate: true }
- useInitialChartContext(): Initialize context with empty options array
- useChartContext(): Access context with dev-time validation
- useRegister(): One-time registration hook for echarts extensions
- Type exports: EChartsCore, EChartExt, Simplify utility type
```

**3. `/src/charts.tsx` - Chart Components**
Implements 7 chart types via `defineChart<T>` factory function:
- **BarChart**: Accepts BarSeriesOption, GridComponentOption
- **EffectScatterChart**: Accepts EffectScatterSeriesOption, GridComponentOption
- **GraphChart**: Accepts GraphSeriesOption
- **HeatmapChart**: Accepts HeatmapSeriesOption
- **LineChart**: Accepts LineSeriesOption, GridComponentOption
- **PieChart**: Accepts PieSeriesOption
- **ScatterChart**: Accepts ScatterSeriesOption, GridComponentOption

Base props for all charts:
```typescript
interface ChartBaseProps<T extends readonly ChartComponentType[]> {
  className?: string
  style?: React.CSSProperties
  containerProps?: React.HTMLAttributes<HTMLDivElement>
  compose?: [...T]  // Compose other components
  use?: EChartExt   // Register additional echarts extensions
  children?: React.ReactNode
  ref?: React.Ref<echarts.ECharts>  // Access echarts instance
}
```
Plus all echarts.EChartsCoreOption properties and composed component options.

**4. `/src/components.tsx` - UI Components**
Implements 14 components via `defineComponent<T>` factory function:
- **Brush**: BrushComponentOption
- **Calendar**: CalendarComponentOption
- **Dataset**: DatasetComponentOption (includes TransformComponent)
- **DataZoom**: DataZoomComponentOption
- **Geo**: GeoComponentOption
- **Graphic**: GraphicComponentOption
- **Legend**: LegendComponentOption
- **MarkArea**: MarkAreaComponentOption
- **MarkLine**: MarkLineComponentOption
- **MarkPoint**: MarkPointComponentOption
- **Matrix**: MatrixComponentOption
- **Polar**: PolarComponentOption
- **SingleAxis**: SingleAxisComponentOption
- **Timeline**: TimelineComponentOption
- **Title**: TitleComponentOption
- **Toolbox**: ToolboxComponentOption
- **Tooltip**: TooltipComponentOption
- **VisualMap**: VisualMapComponentOption

All components: render null, push props to ChartContext, register extensions via echarts.use()

**5. `/src/features.tsx` - Features**
```typescript
export function AxisBreak() {
  useRegister((echarts) => {
    echarts.use(EChartAxisBreak)
  })
  return null
}
```

**6. `/src/global.d.ts` - Type Declarations**
- Declares CSS module: `declare module '*.css'`

---

### Story Files

**Button Component**

`/src/stories/Button.tsx`:
```typescript
export interface ButtonProps {
  primary?: boolean
  backgroundColor?: string
  size?: 'small' | 'medium' | 'large'
  label: string  // Required
  onClick?: (() => void) | undefined
}

export const Button = ({ primary = false, size = 'medium', backgroundColor, label, ...props }: ButtonProps)
```
- Renders `<button type="button">` with conditional CSS classes: `storybook-button`, `storybook-button--{size}`, `storybook-button--{primary ? primary : secondary}`
- Applies backgroundColor inline style

`/src/stories/Button.stories.ts`:
- Title: 'Example/Button'
- Tags: ['autodocs']
- Layout: 'centered'
- Args: { onClick: fn() }
- ArgTypes: { backgroundColor: { control: 'color' } }
- Stories: Primary (primary: true), Secondary (default), Large (size: 'large'), Small (size: 'small')

---

**Header Component**

`/src/stories/Header.tsx`:
```typescript
type User = { name: string }

export interface HeaderProps {
  user?: User | undefined
  onLogin?: (() => void) | undefined
  onLogout?: (() => void) | undefined
  onCreateAccount?: (() => void) | undefined
}

export const Header = ({ user, onLogin, onLogout, onCreateAccount }: HeaderProps)
```
- Renders Acme logo with SVG and h1
- If user exists: shows welcome message + "Log out" button
- If no user: shows "Log in" + "Sign up" buttons

`/src/stories/Header.stories.ts`:
- Title: 'Example/Header'
- Tags: ['autodocs']
- Layout: 'fullscreen'
- Args: { onLogin: fn(), onLogout: fn(), onCreateAccount: fn() }
- Stories: LoggedIn (user: { name: 'Jane Doe' }), LoggedOut (no args)

---

**Page Component**

`/src/stories/Page.tsx`:
```typescript
type User = { name: string }

export const Page: React.FC = () => {
  const [user, setUser] = React.useState<User | undefined>()
  
  return (
    <article>
      <Header ... />
      <section className="storybook-page">
        {/* Content about component-driven development */}
      </section>
    </article>
  )
}
```
- Manages user login state
- Contains Header + informational content about Storybook

`/src/stories/Page.stories.ts`:
- Title: 'Example/Page'
- Layout: 'fullscreen'
- Stories: LoggedOut (static), LoggedIn (with play() interaction test)
- LoggedIn test: clicks login button, verifies logout button appears

---

**Configure Page**

`/src/stories/Configure.mdx`:
- MDX documentation page with styled guide sections
- Covers: Configure your project, Do more with Storybook
- Sections: Styling, Context, Assets, Autodocs, Chromatic, Figma, Testing, A11y, Theming
- Socials section with GitHub, Discord, YouTube, Tutorials links
- Responsive grid layouts (2-col, 4-col, adapts to mobile)

---

### CSS Files

**`/src/stories/button.css`**
- `.storybook-button`: Base button styles (rounded, bold, sans-serif)
- `.storybook-button--primary`: #555ab9 bg, white text
- `.storybook-button--secondary`: Transparent bg, box shadow, #333 text
- Size variants: small (10px 16px, 12px font), medium (11px 20px, 14px), large (12px 24px, 16px)

**`/src/stories/header.css`**
- `.storybook-header`: Flexbox layout, bottom border, padding
- Header SVG: inline-block, vertical-align
- h1: inline-block, vertical margins, 700 weight, 20px font
- Button spacing: 10px margin-left
- Welcome text: 14px, #333 color, 10px margin-right

**`/src/stories/page.css`**
- `.storybook-page`: Max 600px, centered, padding 48px 20px, Nunito Sans
- h2: 32px bold
- Paragraphs, lists: Standard spacing
- `.tip`: Green badge (e7fdd8 bg, 357a14 text, 11px font)
- `.tip-wrapper`: 40px margins, 13px font
- Responsive SVG icons: 12px, blue fill (#1ea7fd)

---

## Key Architectural Patterns

1. **Context-based Chart Composition**: Charts use React Context to collect nested component options, then merge them with `setOption()`
2. **Factory Functions**: Both `defineChart` and `defineComponent` use factory patterns to create reusable component factories
3. **Type-Safe Props**: Heavy use of TypeScript generics to compose prop types from echarts options
4. **Forward Refs**: Charts forward refs to access underlying echarts instance directly
5. **Lazy Registration**: Components register echarts extensions only once via `useRegister` hook
6. **Props Merging**: Chart props include base props + all echarts options + composed component options via `Simplify` utility type

All files are now fully documented with complete interfaces, component signatures, and usage patterns.

- Covers: Configure your project, Do more with Storybook
- Sections: Styling, Context, Assets, Autodocs, Chromatic, Figma, Testing, A11y, Theming
- Socials section with GitHub, Discord, YouTube, Tutorials links
- Responsive grid layouts (2-col, 4-col, adapts to mobile)

---

### CSS Files

**`/src/stories/button.css`**
- `.storybook-button`: Base button styles (rounded, bold, sans-serif)
- `.storybook-button--primary`: #555ab9 bg, white text
- `.storybook-button--secondary`: Transparent bg, box shadow, #333 text
- Size variants: small (10px 16px, 12px font), medium (11px 20px, 14px), large (12px 24px, 16px)

**`/src/stories/header.css`**
- `.storybook-header`: Flexbox layout, bottom border, padding
- Header SVG: inline-block, vertical-align
- h1: inline-block, vertical margins, 700 weight, 20px font
- Button spacing: 10px margin-left
- Welcome text: 14px, #333 color, 10px margin-right

**`/src/stories/page.css`**
- `.storybook-page`: Max 600px, centered, padding 48px 20px, Nunito Sans
- h2: 32px bold
- Paragraphs, lists: Standard spacing
- `.tip`: Green badge (e7fdd8 bg, 357a14 text, 11px font)
- `.tip-wrapper`: 40px margins, 13px font
- Responsive SVG icons: 12px, blue fill (#1ea7fd)

---

## Key Architectural Patterns

1. **Context-based Chart Composition**: Charts use React Context to collect nested component options, then merge them with `setOption()`
2. **Factory Functions**: Both `defineChart` and `defineComponent` use factory patterns to create reusable component factories
3. **Type-Safe Props**: Heavy use of TypeScript generics to compose prop types from echarts options
4. **Forward Refs**: Charts forward refs to access underlying echarts instance directly
5. **Lazy Registration**: Components register echarts extensions only once via `useRegister` hook
6. **Props Merging**: Chart props include base props + all echarts options + composed component options via `Simplify` utility type

All files are now fully documented with complete interfaces, component signatures, and usage patterns.

content.thinking:
OK so the addon-vitest automatically sets up project annotations. The issue is that the `storybook/preview` path in the `import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'` is not exported from this pre-release storybook package. 

But `definePreview` is exported from `@storybook/react-vite`. The preview.tsx should import from there. Let me check if the `#.storybook/preview` import used in stories works or if I should just use a relative path import.

Actually, looking at the setup more carefully, the vitest plugin sets up project annotations automatically from the preview config. 

The `#.storybook/preview` import is a Node.js subpath import that needs to be defined in package.json's `imports` field to work. The error message indicates that `./preview` isn't being exported from the storybook package under the current conditions, so I need to figure out if this is auto-configured or if I need to set it up manually.

Actually, I realize the real issue is that the stories are trying to import from `#.storybook/preview` using a subpath alias, but that's not properly configured. I could either add the subpath import to package.json or just use relative paths instead, which would be simpler. The relative paths would vary depending on where each story file is located, so that could get messy across different directories.

HeatmapChart doesn't include GridComponent - it just has `[EChartHeatmapChart]`. So when using xAxis/yAxis, I need to compose it with another chart that has GridComponent, or pass GridComponent via the `use` prop.

Looking at the `use` prop type: `EChartExt` which is `Parameters<(typeof echarts)['use']>[0]`. I can pass `GridComponent` from `echarts/components` via the `use` prop.

But wait, `GridComponent` isn't exported from `@fanciers/echarts-react`. Let me check what's exported...


The library only exports renderers, chart components, UI components, and the echarts object itself - no GridComponent. So I'll need to import GridComponent directly from echarts/components and pass it through the `use` prop, or take a simpler approach for the story.

Looking at the types more carefully, the `use` prop accepts what echarts.use() accepts, so I can import GridComponent directly and pass it there.

content.text:
Good - Header is fixed. HeatmapChart error is now "Heatmap must use with visualMap". The VisualMap component is a child component that registers via context, but the issue is that VisualMap registers its echarts extension via `useChartContext()` which runs after the chart's `useLayoutEffect`. The VisualMapComponent needs to be registered before the chart renders.

Let me fix this by importing `VisualMapComponent` from echarts directly and passing it via `use`:

input.old_string:
import { GridComponent } from 'echarts/components';
import { CanvasRenderer, HeatmapChart, Title, Tooltip, VisualMap } from '@fanciers/echarts-react';
input.new_string:
import { GridComponent, VisualMapComponent } from 'echarts/components';
import { CanvasRenderer, HeatmapChart, Title, Tooltip, VisualMap } from '@fanciers/echarts-react';

content.thinking:
The issue is that even though I'm registering VisualMapComponent via `use`, the VisualMap child component option doesn't get applied before the chart renders. The error "Heatmap must use with visualMap" means the echarts chart instance needs visualMap configuration to be part of the options when it renders.

The problem is the timing - the child components (like VisualMap) push their options to context in `useLayoutEffect`, but the chart also reads and applies options in `useLayoutEffect`. The order matters.

Let me try passing `visualMap` directly as a prop to HeatmapChart instead of using the VisualMap child component.

content.text: The issue is a timing problem with child components - VisualMap pushes its options via context which happens after chart render. Let me pass `visualMap` directly as a chart prop instead:

content.text:
Two issues:
1. `#.storybook/preview` module not resolved by TypeScript 
2. Implicit `any` types on play function parameters

Let me fix both. For #1, I need to add the path mapping to tsconfig. For #2, I'll avoid destructuring and let TypeScript infer.

content.thinking:
The TypeScript errors are because:
1. The preview.tsx `definePreview` returns a type where `.meta()` produces `never` types since there are no addons configured in the `definePreview`. This seems like a typing issue with the Storybook version.

2. The chart components like `BarChart` are created with `defineChart` which uses `React.forwardRef` and gets cast to `ChartComponentType`. The `meta()` call doesn't know the component props, resulting in `never` types.

Let me check how the `definePreview` return type works, specifically around addons. Looking at the `__definePreview` function, it expects addons:


The function automatically injects several addon exports into the addons array, but the type definition for `__definePreview` seems to be constraining the addon types in a way that causes the parameter types to resolve to `never`, which then cascades through to the `.meta()` return type.

Looking at the actual error more closely, the `component` prop isn't being properly typed because `ChartComponentType` is a complex generic function component, and TypeScript's inference is struggling with it. The `parameters` error showing `Type '{ layout: string; }' is not assignable to type 'never'` confirms the addon typing is the root cause—I should try explicitly passing the addons through the `addons` field instead of spreading them.

content.text: The issue is that `addons` is **required** in the `definePreview` input. Let me update the preview to pass an empty addons array:

content.thinking:
The issue is clear: the chart components (BarChart, etc.) are typed with `ChartComponentType` which uses complex generic types. The `meta()` function can't properly infer the args type from the component, resulting in `args: never`. When `args` is `never`, it requires the `args` property in story calls but can never satisfy it.

The key error is: `Property 'args' is missing in type ... but required in type '{ args: never; }'`

This means `meta.story()` expects `args: never` (because the component type doesn't have standard React props), and overload 1 wants a render function but combined with a play function it doesn't match.


The render function approach should work for the first overload, but adding a play function breaks the type matching. The real problem is that these chart components have an unconventional type signature—they're interfaces with call signatures rather than standard React components, which the CSF Factory types can't handle properly. I could suppress the errors with `as any` or skip passing the component to `meta()` altogether.

Looking more closely at the error, the pattern with both render and play functions is matching overload 2, which expects `args: never`. If I don't set `component` for chart stories, there won't be any inferred props and the args constraint should become more permissive. Let me try removing the component assignment for these chart stories since they're using render functions anyway.

content.text: The chart components use unconventional types (`ChartComponentType`) that don't work well with CSF Factory's type inference. Let me remove `component` from chart story metas since they always use render functions:

content.text:
Everything is working. Here's a summary of what was done:

## Summary

### Preview setup (`.storybook/preview.tsx`)
- Converted to CSF Factory format using `definePreview` from `@storybook/react-vite`
- No global CSS, providers, portals, or MSW needed (this is a pure component library)

### Configuration changes
- Added `#.storybook/*` path mapping to `tsconfig.json` for CSF Factory imports
- Added `imports` field to `package.json` for Node.js subpath import resolution

### Story files created (9 files, 26 stories total)

| File | Stories | Description |
|------|---------|-------------|
| `src/stories/Button.stories.tsx` | 5 | Primary, Secondary, Large, Small, **CssCheck** |
| `src/stories/Header.stories.tsx` | 3 | LoggedIn, LoggedOut, ClickLogin |
| `src/stories/Page.stories.tsx` | 3 | LoggedOut, LoggedIn, LogoutFlow |
| `src/BarChart.stories.tsx` | 3 | Default, Stacked, Horizontal |
| `src/LineChart.stories.tsx` | 3 | Default, MultiSeries, AreaStyle |
| `src/PieChart.stories.tsx` | 3 | Default, Doughnut, RoseChart |
| `src/ScatterChart.stories.tsx` | 2 | Default, BubbleChart |
| `src/GraphChart.stories.tsx` | 2 | Default (force layout), CircularLayout |
| `src/HeatmapChart.stories.tsx` | 2 | Default, SimpleHeatmap |

### Key patterns
- All stories use CSF Factory format (`preview.meta()` / `meta.story()`)
- All stories have `tags: ['ai-generated']`
- Every story has a meaningful `play` function with assertions
- `CssCheck` story verifies `background-color: rgb(85, 90, 185)` from the Button's CSS
- Chart stories use `CanvasRenderer` via the `use` prop
- HeatmapChart passes `GridComponent` and `VisualMapComponent` directly since it doesn't bundle them

Do not skip steps or │ attempt to do them out of order. │ Your goal is to make Storybook fully functional in this project by │ analyzing the codebase, │ configuring the preview with the right decorators, and writing stories │ for some components. │ The end state should be a Storybook where any component — from a small │ button to a full page — can be added without story-specific │ workarounds. All necessary providers, CSS, browser state, and network │ mocks should live in the shared preview so that just rendering the │ component in the story is enough. │ After each created story, run Vitest to verify it renders. │ If the test fails, read the error, fix the issue, and re-run until it │ passes before moving on. │ - Copy real patterns from the codebase │ - Keep the app code unchanged │ - Put the default setup in `.storybook/preview.tsx` │ - Keep app mocking and runtime setup in `.storybook/preview.tsx`, not │ in the stories │ ### Storybook Documentation Reference │ Use the following references to look up Storybook APIs, concepts, or │ examples: │ - Full docs index: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/llms.txthttps://storybook.js.org/llms.txt]8;; │ - See code snippets only with codeOnly=true param e.g. │ ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=ts&codeOnly=truehttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=ts&codeOnly=true]8;; │ Key documentation pages for this task: │ - Writing stories: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories...]8;; │ - Decorators: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/decorators.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/deco...]8;; │ - Args: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/args.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/args.md?re...]8;; │ - Play functions: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/play-function.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/...]8;; │ - Vitest integration: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-tests/vitest-plugin.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-test...]8;; │ Fetch these URLs directly when you need guidance on Storybook APIs or │ patterns. │ ### Step 1: Analyze the codebase │ Read enough of the app to understand the full runtime environment │ before writing any stories. │ Do not stop at `main.tsx` or `App.tsx`. │ Follow imports into providers, pages, hooks, and shared components │ until you know: │ - which providers exist │ - which CSS files are injected │ - which queries fetch data │ - which browser-state reads happen │ - which portals and portal roots exist │ - which pages and components show the real usage patterns │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // src/main.tsx │ import "./index.css"; │ import App from "./App"; │ import { SessionProvider } from "./contexts/SessionContext"; │ createRoot(document.getElementById("root")!).render( │ │ │ , │ ); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should copy: │ - the `index.css` import │ - the `SessionProvider` │ - the same provider order │ Example of tracing the app deeper: │ ```tsx │ // src/App.tsx │ function App() { │ const { products, loadMoreProducts } = useProducts(); │ const { currentUser, signOut } = useSession(); │ // ... │ } │ ``` │ ```ts │ // src/hooks/useProducts.ts │ const response = await fetch(apiBaseUrl + "/products?page=1"); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // src/hooks/useTheme.ts │ const savedTheme = localStorage.getItem("theme"); │ ``` │ That means the default Storybook setup should discover and prepare: │ - provider state │ - MSW handlers for queries │ - browser-state values that are actually read during render │ ### Step 2: Build one default app environment in preview │ Set up Storybook once so most stories work without story-specific │ setup. │ Start with the smallest faithful environment: │ - the real provider tree │ - the real root CSS │ - seeded browser state if the app reads it during render │ - MSW for network/data queries │ It is fine to seed browser state such as `localStorage`, │ `sessionStorage`, and cookies when the app reads them during render. │ Seed only the specific app-owned keys and values you need. │ Do not clear all `localStorage`, `sessionStorage`, or cookies, and do │ not reset Storybook's own state. │ Do not mock or redefine the browser runtime itself. │ The stories run in Vitest browser mode, so the real browser │ environment should already exist. │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles │ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; │ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; │ import { SessionProvider } from '../src/contexts/SessionContext'; │ export default definePreview({ │ decorators: [ │ (Story) => ( │ │ │ │ ), │ ], │ async beforeEach() { │ localStorage.setItem('theme', 'dark'); │ localStorage.setItem('sidebar:open', 'true'); │ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ Use this same idea for: │ - providers │ - root CSS │ - browser state │ - dates, and if the app logic depends on them during render then │ always use `mockdate` │ Example with the `mockdate` package: │ ```tsx │ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; │ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; │ export default definePreview({ │ async beforeEach() { │ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ ### Step 3: Support portals with preview-body.html │ If the app uses portals, copy that setup into Storybook too. │ Look for patterns like: │ - `createPortal(...)` │ - modal, dialog, drawer, popover, tooltip, toast, or dropdown portal │ components │ - hard-coded roots such as `#portal-root`, `#modal-root`, │ `#drawer-root`, or `#toast-root` │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // real component │ return createPortal(, │ document.getElementById("portal-root")!); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should create the same portal root in │ `.storybook/preview-body.html`: │ ```html │ │
│ ``` │ If the app uses multiple portal roots, create all of them there: │ ```html │ │
│
│
│ ``` │ If a library portals directly to `document.body`, do not add extra │ roots for it. │ Make sure the copied page shell, CSS, and layout still allow overlays, │ fixed positioning, and z-index stacking to render correctly. │ ### Step 4: Mock side effects globally │ All network/data queries should be handled by the default Storybook │ environment. │ - Always use `msw-storybook-addon` for query mocking. │ - If you introduce MSW, run `npx msw init ./public --save` to create │ the worker file. │ - Make sure Storybook serves `./public` as a static dir so │ `mockServiceWorker.js` is available. │ - Do not mock `fetch` directly. │ - Network/data queries should return deterministic mock data. │ - If you need to change dependencies, first check the lockfile and use │ that package manager for the change. │ Example of copying a real fetch pattern into shared handlers: │ ```ts │ // real app hook │ const response = await fetch( │ apiBaseUrl + │ "/products?" + │ new URLSearchParams({ │ page: "1", │ sort: "featured", │ }), │ ); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/msw-handlers.ts │ import { http, HttpResponse } from "msw"; │ export const mswHandlers = { │ products: [ │ http.get("]8;;https://api.example.com/products",https://api.example.com/products",]8;; │ () => │ HttpResponse.json({ │ items: [ │ { │ id: "product-1", │ name: "Example product", │ description: "Mock product description", │ imageUrl: │ "]8;;https://images.example.com/product.jpg",https://images.example.com/product.jpg",]8;; │ price: 42, │ }, │ ], │ }), │ ), │ ], │ }; │ ``` │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; │ import { initialize, mswLoader } from 'msw-storybook-addon'; │ import { mswHandlers } from './msw-handlers'; │ initialize({ │ onUnhandledRequest: 'bypass', │ }); │ export default definePreview({ │ loaders: [mswLoader], │ parameters: { │ msw: { │ handlers: mswHandlers, │ }, │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/main.ts │ import type { StorybookConfig } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ const config: StorybookConfig = { │ staticDirs: ["../public"], │ }; │ export default config; │ ``` │ Keep these mocks global. │ Do not put fetch mocks in individual stories. │ Only add handlers for requests that the shared preview setup or the │ stories actually use. │ Do not add catch-all handlers that can hide unrelated failures. │ If the defaults are not enough, improve the shared default setup │ instead. │ Seed browser state when needed, but do not mock `window`, `document`, │ `navigator`, observers, or similar runtime APIs. │ The only exception is `mockdate` when date-based rendering exists. │ ### Step 5: Write stories │ Try to find around 10 good candidate components for story files. │ Write colocated stories for top-level components, from low-level │ reusable components up to page components. │ Write up to 10 story files, or fewer only if the codebase clearly has │ fewer meaningful targets. │ The stories should use JSX copied from real usage patterns in: │ - pages │ - app shells │ - routes │ - tests │ - existing feature code │ As a rule of thumb, each story file should have around 3 story exports │ when the component or page has enough meaningful states. │ It can have more when the real usage supports it, up to 10 story │ exports in one file. │ Always show all imports explicitly in story and preview files. │ Do not rely on omitted or implied imports in examples or generated │ code. │ #### Story tags │ Every story meta must include the `ai-generated` tag to identify │ AI-created stories: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { SomeComponent } from './SomeComponent'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const Default = meta.story({ │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ If a story could not be fully fixed after the self-healing loop (the │ test still fails │ or the rendering is incomplete), add the `needs-work` tag alongside │ `ai-generated`: │ ```ts │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated', 'needs-work'], │ }); │ ``` │ #### Args vs render │ For simple components where props drive the state, prefer `args` │ stories — no `render` function needed: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { Button } from './Button'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: Button, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const Primary = meta.story({ │ args: { │ variant: 'primary', │ children: 'Save', │ }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i │ })).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }); │ export const Disabled = meta.story({ │ args: { │ variant: 'primary', │ disabled: true, │ children: 'Save', │ }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeDisabled(); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ Use `render` when the story needs composition — wrapping the component │ in layout, combining multiple components, or passing children as JSX: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { Button } from './Button'; │ import { Card } from './Card'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: Button, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const InsideCard = meta.story({ │ render: () => ( │ │ │ │ ), │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i │ })).toBeVisible(); │ await userEvent.click(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i })); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ Keep app mocking and runtime setup in preview, not in the stories. │ Do not build large story-specific harnesses. │ Do not write story files for subcomponents, hooks, contexts, or │ helpers. │ Do not create new application components. │ Do not add a custom `title`. │ Do not stop after only a few easy targets if the codebase has more │ meaningful components or pages available. │ ### Step 6: Write a play function for every story │ Every named story export must have a `play` function. │ The `play` function is not optional, even for simple stories. │ The purpose of the `play` function is to prove that the story actually │ works in the copied Storybook environment: │ - the story renders something real and non-empty │ - the decorators provide the needed context │ - the CSS is applied well enough for the intended state to be visible │ - the MSW mocks or seeded browser state are actually being used │ - important interactions, async loading states, and portals behave │ correctly │ Use `play` functions to verify behavior, not just to click around. │ A story without assertions is incomplete. │ Use tools from `storybook/test` such as: │ - `expect` │ - `waitFor` │ Prefer `canvas` and `userEvent` from the `play` context. │ Do not destructure `canvasElement` just to create `const canvas = │ within(canvasElement)`. │ Do not import `userEvent` from `storybook/test`; use `userEvent` from │ the `play` context instead. │ Only use `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when you need to query outside │ the canvas, such as for portals. │ Example: │ ```tsx │ import type { StoryObj } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ export const FilledForm: Story = { │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ const emailInput = canvas.getByLabelText("email", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(emailInput, "example-email@email.com", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const passwordInput = canvas.getByLabelText("password", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(passwordInput, "ExamplePassword", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const submitButton = canvas.getByRole("button"); │ await userEvent.click(submitButton); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ The assertions should match the real pattern you copied: │ - for provider-backed stories, assert the provider-dependent UI │ appears correctly │ - for mocked-data stories, wait for the mocked data to appear and │ assert on it │ - for CSS-sensitive states, assert on visibility, text layout, │ class-driven states, or meaningful computed styles │ - for routing or navigation stories, assert the routed state or │ navigation outcome │ - for portal stories, query from `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when │ the UI renders outside the canvas │ Examples of useful checks: │ - a themed button has the expected label and is visibly enabled or │ disabled │ - a modal opened through a decorator or provider is visible in the │ portal root │ - mocked API data appears in the page instead of a loading spinner │ forever │ - a selected tab actually shows the selected panel │ - a toast, alert, or badge has the expected accessible text and visual │ state │ - a CSS class or computed style confirms the real state that matters │ ### Step 7: Prove CSS is loaded in exactly one story named `CssCheck` │ In exactly one story, named `CssCheck`, assert a component-specific │ computed style. `toBeVisible` passes on an unstyled component; a │ concrete style value proves the shared preview loaded the app's CSS. │ Pick a visually distinctive component, read a styling value from its │ source, and assert it with `getComputedStyle`: │ ```tsx │ export const CssCheck: Story = { │ args: { children: "Submit" }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ const button = canvas.getByRole("button", { name: /submit/i }); │ // PrimaryButton uses bg-blue-600 — fails if Tailwind / global CSS did │ not load. │ await expect(getComputedStyle(button).backgroundColor).toBe("rgb(37, │ 99, 235)"); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ ### Step 8: Cover the patterns you found │ Write stories for the real patterns in the codebase, for example: │ - a low-level reusable component in real JSX usage │ - a provider-backed component │ - a browser-state-backed component │ - a fetched-data component │ - a real page component │ Use `App.tsx` to inspect the real provider tree and usage patterns, │ but do not make a story for `App` when the codebase has actual page │ components. │ Example page story: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { ProductPage } from './ProductPage'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: ProductPage, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const Default = meta.story({ │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect( │ canvas.getByRole('heading', { name: /products/i }), │ ).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ ### Step 9: Verify both rendering and types │ As you work, verify the stories with Vitest: │ ```bash │ npx vitest --project storybook │ ``` │ Also verify types so you catch missing required props, broken imports, │ and preview typing issues. Run the same TypeScript command the project │ itself uses. │ ```bash │ │ ``` │ After verification passes, review every changed file and remove │ anything that is not needed for the final solution, especially debug │ fixes, overly broad mocks, unnecessary dependencies, and eval │ artifacts. │ Keep iterating until: │ - every story you wrote passes │ - every story you wrote has a meaningful passing `play` function │ - the changed stories and preview setup pass the project's real │ TypeScript check │ - the rendered output looks sensible │ - the default global mocked environment is strong enough that stories │ do not need manual fetch overrides │ - stories no longer fail because the shared preview setup and story │ JSX are fixed │ - all passing stories have `tags: ['ai-generated']` in their meta │ - any stories that still need work have `tags: ['ai-generated', │ 'needs-work']` in their meta

Pages in Storybook

31→

32→ Render pages with mock data. This makes it easy to build and review page states without 33→ needing to navigate to them in your app. Here are some handy patterns for managing page 34→ data in Storybook: 35→

36→

Perfect! Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the codebase. Let me create a detailed report:

## Comprehensive Codebase Analysis

### 1. Project Structure

**Root Directory:** `/Users/yannbraga/open-source/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-28T22-41-15Z-41b8dcb0/project`

**Key Directories:**
- `src/` - Main source code
  - `stories/` - Storybook stories and example components
    - `assets/` - Image and SVG assets
  - Root level source files
- `.storybook/` - Storybook configuration
  - `eval-support/` - Evaluation support files
  - `eval-results/` - Evaluation results
- `tests/` - Test files
- `.github/workflows/` - GitHub Actions workflows
- `.vscode/` - VS Code configuration

**Key Root Files:**
- `package.json` - Project metadata and dependencies
- `tsconfig.json` - TypeScript configuration
- `vite.config.mts` - Vite build configuration
- `vitest.config.mts` - Vitest test configuration
- `tsdown.config.mts` - TypeScript bundler configuration
- `pnpm-lock.yaml` - pnpm lock file
- `.npmrc`, `.gitignore` - Configuration files
- `README.md` - Project documentation

### 2. Entry Point Analysis

**No Traditional Entry Point (main.tsx/App.tsx)** - This is a **component library**, not an application. The project publishes reusable UI components through a public npm package (`@fanciers/echarts-react`).

**Actual Entry Point:**
- **File:** `/Users/yannbraga/open-source/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-28T22-41-15Z-41b8dcb0/project/src/index.ts`
- **Purpose:** Exports all public API from the library
- **Contents:**
  ```typescript
  export { CanvasRenderer, SVGRenderer } from 'echarts/renderers';
  export { BarChart, EffectScatterChart, GraphChart, HeatmapChart, LineChart, PieChart, ScatterChart } from './charts.js';
  export { Brush, Calendar, Dataset, DataZoom, Geo, Graphic, Legend, MarkArea, MarkLine, MarkPoint, Matrix, Polar, SingleAxis, Timeline, Title, Toolbox, Tooltip, VisualMap } from './components.js';
  export { AxisBreak } from './features.js';
  export { echarts } from './shared.js';
  ```

### 3. Core Architecture

**Project Type:** React component library for ECharts visualization components

**No Providers or Global Setup** - This is a library, not an app. Components are self-contained and designed to be imported into other applications.

### 4. Context Providers

**Single Context: ChartContext**
- **File:** `/Users/yannbraga/open-source/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-28T22-41-15Z-41b8dcb0/project/src/shared.tsx`
- **Purpose:** Manages chart options across child components
- **Interface:**
  ```typescript
  interface ChartContextValue {
    options: echarts.EChartsCoreOption[];
  }
  ```
- **Hooks:**
  - `useInitialChartContext()` - Creates initial context state
  - `useChartContext()` - Consumes context (throws in dev if not within ChartContext)
  - `useRegister(fn)` - Registers ECharts extensions
- **No Redux, Zustand, or other state management libraries**

### 5. Data Fetching Hooks

**No HTTP Data Fetching Hooks** - This is a presentational component library. No fetch, axios, SWR, or React Query imports found.

**Hook Summary:**
- `useInitialChartContext()` - State initialization
- `useChartContext()` - Context consumption
- `useRegister(fn)` - ECharts module registration

### 6. Browser State Hooks

**No Browser State Reading** - No hooks for:
- localStorage
- sessionStorage
- Cookies
- History API

All components are stateless/pure presentational except for the Page story which uses `React.useState` for demo purposes.

### 7. Portal Usage

**No Portal Usage** - No `createPortal()` calls found in any component. No modal systems or portal roots configured.

### 8. CSS Setup

**CSS Approach:** Plain CSS files (no Tailwind, CSS-in-JS, or CSS Modules)

**CSS Files in Stories:**
- `/Users/yannbraga/open-source/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-28T22-41-15Z-41b8dcb0/project/src/stories/button.css` - Button component styles
- `/Users/yannbraga/open-source/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-28T22-41-15Z-41b8dcb0/project/src/stories/header.css` - Header component styles
- `/Users/yannbraga/open-source/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-28T22-41-15Z-41b8dcb0/project/src/stories/page.css` - Page component styles

**Global CSS Declaration:** `/Users/yannbraga/open-source/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-28T22-41-15Z-41b8dcb0/project/src/global.d.ts`
```typescript
declare module '*.css';
```

### 9. Page-Level Components

This is a library, not an app, but the `stories/` directory contains example/demo components:

**Page Components (in `/src/stories/`):**
1. **Page.tsx** - Full page example combining Header and content
   - Uses `React.useState` for demo state management
   - Located at: `/Users/yannbraga/open-source/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-28T22-41-15Z-41b8dcb0/project/src/stories/Page.tsx`

2. **Header.tsx** - Header/navigation example
   - Accepts user, onLogin, onLogout, onCreateAccount props
   - Located at: `/Users/yannbraga/open-source/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-28T22-41-15Z-41b8dcb0/project/src/stories/Header.tsx`

3. **Button.tsx** - Basic button component
   - Located at: `/Users/yannbraga/open-source/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-28T22-41-15Z-41b8dcb0/project/src/stories/Button.tsx`

### 10. Reusable UI Components

**Library Components (Exported from `/src/`):**

**Chart Components:**
- `BarChart` - Bar chart wrapper
- `LineChart` - Line chart wrapper
- `PieChart` - Pie chart wrapper
- `ScatterChart` - Scatter plot wrapper
- `EffectScatterChart` - Effect scatter visualization
- `GraphChart` - Graph/network visualization
- `HeatmapChart` - Heatmap visualization

**Component Wrappers (for ECharts features):**
- `Brush` - Brush selector component
- `Calendar` - Calendar component
- `Dataset` - Data transformation
- `DataZoom` - Data zoom/pan
- `Geo` - Geographic map
- `Graphic` - Custom graphics
- `Legend` - Legend display
- `MarkArea` - Mark area overlay
- `MarkLine` - Mark line overlay
- `MarkPoint` - Mark point overlay
- `Matrix` - Matrix visualization
- `Polar` - Polar coordinate system
- `SingleAxis` - Single axis
- `Timeline` - Timeline control
- `Title` - Chart title
- `Toolbox` - Toolbar
- `Tooltip` - Tooltip display
- `VisualMap` - Visual mapping

**Story Example Components:**
- `Button` - Basic button in `/src/stories/Button.tsx`
- `Header` - Header with auth UI in `/src/stories/Header.tsx`
- `Page` - Full page example in `/src/stories/Page.tsx`

### 11. Package Manager

**pnpm** (version 10.30.1)
- Lockfile: `pnpm-lock.yaml` (9.0 format)
- Config: `.npmrc`

**Key Dependencies:**
- `react` ^19.2.4
- `react-dom` ^19.2.4

- `typescript` ^5.9.3

- `@vitest/browser-playwright` 4.0.18
- `@chromatic-com/storybook` ^5.1.1

### 12. TypeScript Configuration

**File:** `/Users/yannbraga/open-source/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-28T22-41-15Z-41b8dcb0/project/tsconfig.json`

Perfect! I have now read all the source files you requested. Here's a comprehensive summary of the complete codebase:

## Complete Source File Summary

### Core Files

**1. `/src/index.ts` - Main Entry Point**
- Exports all chart components (BarChart, EffectScatterChart, GraphChart, HeatmapChart, LineChart, PieChart, ScatterChart)
- Exports all UI components (Brush, Calendar, Dataset, DataZoom, Geo, Graphic, Legend, MarkArea, MarkLine, MarkPoint, Matrix, Polar, SingleAxis, Timeline, Title, Toolbox, Tooltip, VisualMap)
- Exports AxisBreak feature
- Exports echarts core and renderers (CanvasRenderer, SVGRenderer)
- Exports the echarts object itself for direct access

**2. `/src/shared.tsx` - Shared Context & Utilities**
```typescript
- ChartContextValue interface: { options: echarts.EChartsCoreOption[] }
- ChartContext: React Context for chart options management
- defaultSetOptionOpt: { notMerge: false, lazyUpdate: true }
- useInitialChartContext(): Initialize context with empty options array
- useChartContext(): Access context with dev-time validation
- useRegister(): One-time registration hook for echarts extensions
- Type exports: EChartsCore, EChartExt, Simplify utility type
```

**3. `/src/charts.tsx` - Chart Components**
Implements 7 chart types via `defineChart<T>` factory function:
- **BarChart**: Accepts BarSeriesOption, GridComponentOption
- **EffectScatterChart**: Accepts EffectScatterSeriesOption, GridComponentOption
- **GraphChart**: Accepts GraphSeriesOption
- **HeatmapChart**: Accepts HeatmapSeriesOption
- **LineChart**: Accepts LineSeriesOption, GridComponentOption
- **PieChart**: Accepts PieSeriesOption
- **ScatterChart**: Accepts ScatterSeriesOption, GridComponentOption

Base props for all charts:
```typescript
interface ChartBaseProps<T extends readonly ChartComponentType[]> {
  className?: string
  style?: React.CSSProperties
  containerProps?: React.HTMLAttributes<HTMLDivElement>
  compose?: [...T]  // Compose other components
  use?: EChartExt   // Register additional echarts extensions
  children?: React.ReactNode
  ref?: React.Ref<echarts.ECharts>  // Access echarts instance
}
```
Plus all echarts.EChartsCoreOption properties and composed component options.

**4. `/src/components.tsx` - UI Components**
Implements 14 components via `defineComponent<T>` factory function:
- **Brush**: BrushComponentOption
- **Calendar**: CalendarComponentOption
- **Dataset**: DatasetComponentOption (includes TransformComponent)
- **DataZoom**: DataZoomComponentOption
- **Geo**: GeoComponentOption
- **Graphic**: GraphicComponentOption
- **Legend**: LegendComponentOption
- **MarkArea**: MarkAreaComponentOption
- **MarkLine**: MarkLineComponentOption
- **MarkPoint**: MarkPointComponentOption
- **Matrix**: MatrixComponentOption
- **Polar**: PolarComponentOption
- **SingleAxis**: SingleAxisComponentOption
- **Timeline**: TimelineComponentOption
- **Title**: TitleComponentOption
- **Toolbox**: ToolboxComponentOption
- **Tooltip**: TooltipComponentOption
- **VisualMap**: VisualMapComponentOption

All components: render null, push props to ChartContext, register extensions via echarts.use()

**5. `/src/features.tsx` - Features**
```typescript
export function AxisBreak() {
  useRegister((echarts) => {
    echarts.use(EChartAxisBreak)
  })
  return null
}
```

**6. `/src/global.d.ts` - Type Declarations**
- Declares CSS module: `declare module '*.css'`

---

### Story Files

**Button Component**

`/src/stories/Button.tsx`:
```typescript
export interface ButtonProps {
  primary?: boolean
  backgroundColor?: string
  size?: 'small' | 'medium' | 'large'
  label: string  // Required
  onClick?: (() => void) | undefined
}

export const Button = ({ primary = false, size = 'medium', backgroundColor, label, ...props }: ButtonProps)
```
- Renders `<button type="button">` with conditional CSS classes: `storybook-button`, `storybook-button--{size}`, `storybook-button--{primary ? primary : secondary}`
- Applies backgroundColor inline style

`/src/stories/Button.stories.ts`:
- Title: 'Example/Button'
- Tags: ['autodocs']
- Layout: 'centered'
- Args: { onClick: fn() }
- ArgTypes: { backgroundColor: { control: 'color' } }
- Stories: Primary (primary: true), Secondary (default), Large (size: 'large'), Small (size: 'small')

---

**Header Component**

`/src/stories/Header.tsx`:
```typescript
type User = { name: string }

export interface HeaderProps {
  user?: User | undefined
  onLogin?: (() => void) | undefined
  onLogout?: (() => void) | undefined
  onCreateAccount?: (() => void) | undefined
}

export const Header = ({ user, onLogin, onLogout, onCreateAccount }: HeaderProps)
```
- Renders Acme logo with SVG and h1
- If user exists: shows welcome message + "Log out" button
- If no user: shows "Log in" + "Sign up" buttons

`/src/stories/Header.stories.ts`:
- Title: 'Example/Header'
- Tags: ['autodocs']
- Layout: 'fullscreen'
- Args: { onLogin: fn(), onLogout: fn(), onCreateAccount: fn() }
- Stories: LoggedIn (user: { name: 'Jane Doe' }), LoggedOut (no args)

---

**Page Component**

`/src/stories/Page.tsx`:
```typescript
type User = { name: string }

export const Page: React.FC = () => {
  const [user, setUser] = React.useState<User | undefined>()
  
  return (
    <article>
      <Header ... />
      <section className="storybook-page">
        {/* Content about component-driven development */}
      </section>
    </article>
  )
}
```
- Manages user login state
- Contains Header + informational content about Storybook

`/src/stories/Page.stories.ts`:
- Title: 'Example/Page'
- Layout: 'fullscreen'
- Stories: LoggedOut (static), LoggedIn (with play() interaction test)
- LoggedIn test: clicks login button, verifies logout button appears

---

**Configure Page**

`/src/stories/Configure.mdx`:
- MDX documentation page with styled guide sections
- Covers: Configure your project, Do more with Storybook
- Sections: Styling, Context, Assets, Autodocs, Chromatic, Figma, Testing, A11y, Theming
- Socials section with GitHub, Discord, YouTube, Tutorials links
- Responsive grid layouts (2-col, 4-col, adapts to mobile)

---

### CSS Files

**`/src/stories/button.css`**
- `.storybook-button`: Base button styles (rounded, bold, sans-serif)
- `.storybook-button--primary`: #555ab9 bg, white text
- `.storybook-button--secondary`: Transparent bg, box shadow, #333 text
- Size variants: small (10px 16px, 12px font), medium (11px 20px, 14px), large (12px 24px, 16px)

**`/src/stories/header.css`**
- `.storybook-header`: Flexbox layout, bottom border, padding
- Header SVG: inline-block, vertical-align
- h1: inline-block, vertical margins, 700 weight, 20px font
- Button spacing: 10px margin-left
- Welcome text: 14px, #333 color, 10px margin-right

**`/src/stories/page.css`**
- `.storybook-page`: Max 600px, centered, padding 48px 20px, Nunito Sans
- h2: 32px bold
- Paragraphs, lists: Standard spacing
- `.tip`: Green badge (e7fdd8 bg, 357a14 text, 11px font)
- `.tip-wrapper`: 40px margins, 13px font
- Responsive SVG icons: 12px, blue fill (#1ea7fd)

---

## Key Architectural Patterns

1. **Context-based Chart Composition**: Charts use React Context to collect nested component options, then merge them with `setOption()`
2. **Factory Functions**: Both `defineChart` and `defineComponent` use factory patterns to create reusable component factories
3. **Type-Safe Props**: Heavy use of TypeScript generics to compose prop types from echarts options
4. **Forward Refs**: Charts forward refs to access underlying echarts instance directly
5. **Lazy Registration**: Components register echarts extensions only once via `useRegister` hook
6. **Props Merging**: Chart props include base props + all echarts options + composed component options via `Simplify` utility type

All files are now fully documented with complete interfaces, component signatures, and usage patterns.

Pages in Storybook

24→

25→ We recommend building UIs with a{' '} 26→ 27→ component-driven 28→ {' '} 29→ process starting with atomic components and ending with pages. 30→

31→

32→ Render pages with mock data. This makes it easy to build and review page states without 33→ needing to navigate to them in your app. Here are some handy patterns for managing page 34→ data in Storybook: 35→

36→
    37→
  • 38→ Use a higher-level connected component. Storybook helps you compose such data from the 39→ "args" of child component stories 40→
    • 41→
  • 42→ Assemble data in the page component from your services. You can mock these services out 43→ using Storybook. 44→
    • 45→

content.text:
Now I have a thorough understanding of the codebase. This is an ECharts React component library with:
- Chart components (BarChart, LineChart, PieChart, etc.)
- UI components (Title, Tooltip, Legend, etc.) used as children of charts
- Example components (Button, Header, Page) in `src/stories/`
- No global CSS to import, no providers needed, no data fetching, no portals
- pnpm package manager, strict TypeScript

Let me fetch the CSF Factory docs and then start building.

But wait - the error says "Vitest failed to find the current suite", which is a vitest runtime error, not a CSF parsing error. Let me check if there's another issue. Let me try running the test with more verbose output.

content.text:
Good - Header is fixed. HeatmapChart error is now "Heatmap must use with visualMap". The VisualMap component is a child component that registers via context, but the issue is that VisualMap registers its echarts extension via `useChartContext()` which runs after the chart's `useLayoutEffect`. The VisualMapComponent needs to be registered before the chart renders.

Let me fix this by importing `VisualMapComponent` from echarts directly and passing it via `use`:

input.old_string:
import { GridComponent } from 'echarts/components';
import { CanvasRenderer, HeatmapChart, Title, Tooltip, VisualMap } from '@fanciers/echarts-react';
input.new_string:
import { GridComponent, VisualMapComponent } from 'echarts/components';
import { CanvasRenderer, HeatmapChart, Title, Tooltip, VisualMap } from '@fanciers/echarts-react';

content.text:
Everything is working. Here's a summary of what was done:

## Summary

### Preview setup (`.storybook/preview.tsx`)
- Converted to CSF Factory format using `definePreview` from `@storybook/react-vite`
- No global CSS, providers, portals, or MSW needed (this is a pure component library)

### Configuration changes
- Added `#.storybook/*` path mapping to `tsconfig.json` for CSF Factory imports
- Added `imports` field to `package.json` for Node.js subpath import resolution

### Story files created (9 files, 26 stories total)

| File | Stories | Description |
|------|---------|-------------|
| `src/stories/Button.stories.tsx` | 5 | Primary, Secondary, Large, Small, **CssCheck** |
| `src/stories/Header.stories.tsx` | 3 | LoggedIn, LoggedOut, ClickLogin |
| `src/stories/Page.stories.tsx` | 3 | LoggedOut, LoggedIn, LogoutFlow |
| `src/BarChart.stories.tsx` | 3 | Default, Stacked, Horizontal |
| `src/LineChart.stories.tsx` | 3 | Default, MultiSeries, AreaStyle |
| `src/PieChart.stories.tsx` | 3 | Default, Doughnut, RoseChart |
| `src/ScatterChart.stories.tsx` | 2 | Default, BubbleChart |
| `src/GraphChart.stories.tsx` | 2 | Default (force layout), CircularLayout |
| `src/HeatmapChart.stories.tsx` | 2 | Default, SimpleHeatmap |

### Key patterns
- All stories use CSF Factory format (`preview.meta()` / `meta.story()`)
- All stories have `tags: ['ai-generated']`
- Every story has a meaningful `play` function with assertions
- `CssCheck` story verifies `background-color: rgb(85, 90, 185)` from the Button's CSS
- Chart stories use `CanvasRenderer` via the `use` prop
- HeatmapChart passes `GridComponent` and `VisualMapComponent` directly since it doesn't bundle them
